summary: Platts: NE Asia PP yarn/inj +10, BOPP +80, block copo +95 USD/ton w/w
PetroChina L5E89 yarn 1,115 CIF Hai Phong / 1,125 HCMC (up 85)
Hanwha settled PP injection at 1,000 USD/ton CIF, below other origins
paras: According to Platts assessments, benchmark prices for PP yarn/injection in Northeast Asia rose USD 10/ton week-on-week, BOPP surged USD 80/ton, and PP block copolymer rose USD 95/ton. In Southeast Asia, PP yarn/injection rose USD 15/ton to USD 1,020/ton CFR; IPP Film also rose USD 15/ton to USD 1,070/ton CFR; BOPP rose USD 15/ton to USD 1,040/ton CFR; and PP block copolymer surged USD 65/ton to USD 1,070/ton CFR.

According to assessments from another international agency, PP prices across Northeast Asia rose uniformly by USD 30/ton week-on-week, while PP prices in Southeast Asia and South Asia each rose USD 10–20/ton.

Overall, the Asian PP market saw prices recover this week after several consecutive weeks of weakness. The rebound was driven by a surge in crude oil to around USD 85/barrel. PP supply in China was affected as many producers remained under maintenance and restarted more slowly than initially expected in late June/early July. In Southeast Asia, however, ample domestic supply and weak buying sentiment continued to restrain the PP price rise.

Notably, in Vietnam, most PP sellers reported a sharp rise in buying enquiries, though most buyers took only small volumes and only when sellers accepted a modest increase over the previous week. Many buyers still held about a month's worth of inventory and were in no rush to buy, partly because they expect this price rebound to be short-lived and prices likely to ease again in the near term.

Today, 17 July, the import offer for PP yarn L5E89 of PetroChina (China) to Vietnam stood at USD 1,115/ton CIF Hai Phong and USD 1,125/ton CIF HCMC, up a sharp USD 85/ton week-on-week. The offer for BOPP L5D98 rose USD 65–70/ton to USD 1,110–1,120/ton CIF. This is a fairly reasonable level, as the FOB price for PP homo in China this week is USD 1,090–1,100/ton. Another distributor offered PP yarn/injection/BOPP of Jinneng to Vietnam at USD 1,150–1,160/ton CIF, but buyers bid significantly lower.

For Korean material, Lotte Chemical's PP yarn offer to Vietnam rose fairly sharply this week to USD 1,070/ton CIF, though firm selling prices (grades J-150H, J-160H, J-170H, H5300, H1500) were recorded as low as USD 1,020/ton CIF. Lotte's firm selling price for PP Film (IPP) H4540 to Vietnamese buyers was recorded at USD 1,070/ton CIF.

A distributor for Hanwha (Korea) reportedly settled a PP injection sale to Vietnamese buyers this week at USD 1,000/ton CIF, notably lower than PP of other origins. However, the volume was small — only about 300 tons — and the distributor has since sold out. Thus, this week's PP offer and firm selling prices for Korean-origin material are notably lower than Chinese-origin material.

In domestic trading, a survey today found offer and transaction prices for PP yarn/injection in the south commonly at VND 31,000–32,700/kg incl. VAT, depending on origin and grade — up sharply by about VND 2,000/kg versus the end of last week. For example, at the end of last week, PP yarn F501 of Hyosung Vina was recorded as low as VND 29,100/kg incl. VAT, whereas today most sellers offered VND 31,000–31,200/kg incl. VAT.

Similarly, in the north, most trading sellers offered PP yarn 114G and PP injection 105G of NSRP up to VND 32,000/kg incl. VAT for prompt delivery, up about VND 2,500/kg versus the end of last week. For PP 114G with late-July/early-August delivery, offers were VND 31,300–31,500/kg incl. VAT. One distributor offered PP yarn UH450J of Daelim origin and PP yarn HD500 of S-Oil (Korea) origin both at VND 31,000/kg incl. VAT, delivered in the north. One distributor offered PP injection P700J of SCG (Thailand) origin at VND 32,500/kg incl. VAT at Hai Phong port. PP Film B1101 (laminating) of IRPC (Thailand) origin was also offered at VND 31,000/kg incl. VAT.

While the PP homo uptrend paused at the end of the week, PP copolymer offers from some distributors edged back up today (PP copo prices fell early this week, then held flat from midweek). For example, in the south, Hyosung Vina's PP copo offer rose again to VND 34,500–35,000/kg incl. VAT after dropping sharply to VND 34,000/kg incl. VAT earlier in the week. As of today, Hyosung Vina's plant is reported to have temporarily suspended offers.
For PP coating, Lotte (Korea) M9600 was offered in the south at VND 42,000–43,000/kg incl. VAT. In the north, few sellers held prompt M9600 stock, creating a slight shortage.

summary: Platts: NE Asia HDPE all grades +30, LLDPE +10, LDPE +70 USD/ton
PetroChina LLDPE 7042N/2020F jumped 115 to 1,055-1,065 USD/ton CIF
Domestic PE uptrend paused today; some HDPE offered slightly lower
paras: According to Platts assessments, in Northeast Asia the benchmark prices for HDPE Film/yarn/injection/blow all rose by USD 30/ton week-on-week, LLDPE gained USD 10/ton, and LDPE climbed USD 70/ton. In Southeast Asia, prices for all grades of LDPE, LLDPE and HDPE held flat against the previous week; HDPE Film/yarn/blow/injection ranged from USD 1,050–1,060/ton CFR, LLDPE stood at USD 1,010/ton CFR, LLDPE metallocene C6 at USD 1,160/ton CFR, and LDPE at USD 1,350/ton CFR.

According to assessments from another international agency, benchmark prices for HDPE Film/yarn/blow/injection in Northeast Asia rose USD 20/ton week-on-week, while prices in Southeast Asia and South Asia held flat; LDPE Film in Northeast Asia rose USD 10–20/ton but held flat in Southeast Asia (ranging USD 1,200–1,300/ton CFR) and fell USD 30/ton in India; LLDPE Film in Northeast Asia rose USD 20/ton and in Southeast Asia rose USD 30–50/ton (to USD 950–1,100/ton CFR), while South Asia was flat.

Overall, the Asian PE market this week ranged from flat to higher versus the previous week. The market was supported by a surge in crude oil futures (pushing up ethylene and naphtha feedstock costs), but relatively weak demand from downstream industries restrained overall trading activity. LLDPE posted the clearest gains, while HDPE was the most stable. In Southeast Asia, buying enquiries increased for August cargoes as buyers grew concerned about securing supply at current prices amid the surge in feedstock costs.

In Vietnam, as of today, 17 July, the import offer for LLDPE Film 7042N and 2020F from PetroChina (China) jumped by as much as USD 115/ton versus the middle of last week, reaching USD 1,055–1,065/ton CIF Hai Phong.

Earlier this week, the distributor for Lotte Titan (Malaysia) quoted a firm selling price for LDPE Film 260GG to Vietnamese buyers at USD 1,100/ton CIF and quickly sold out, prioritizing manufacturing customers. Today, Lotte Titan quoted off-grade (OG) LDPE Film 260GG to Vietnam at USD 1,120/ton CIF, higher than the firm selling price for prime LDPE Film earlier in the week — reflecting the fairly strong rise in PE prices during the week.

One distributor offered HDPE Film 7000F of Marlex (USA) origin to Vietnam at USD 1,040/ton CIF HCMC, with shipment from the USA in August 2026. While the offer is highly competitive, the long lead time on US cargoes (expected to arrive in Vietnam around October–November 2026) has left buyers less enthusiastic.

In domestic trading this week, traders pushed hard to raise prices, expecting offer prices to follow the upward momentum in ethylene. The rising prices prompted buyers to quickly lock in orders on concerns that next week's transaction levels could be higher. The distributor for Sibur (Russia) shared that its PE sales volume in the Vietnamese market continued to improve strongly this week and stock was nearly sold out.

After several consecutive days of price increases, the PE uptrend paused today, with some sellers even offering certain HDPE grades slightly lower to speed up sales. For example, in the south, one distributor offered HDPE yarn 5000S and 7750Z (Luqing) of Chinese origin at VND 32,900/kg incl. VAT; HDPE yarn EA53010 of Reliance (India) origin and HDPE 3301 of SK (Korea) origin were both offered at VND 32,900/kg incl. VAT; and HDPE yarn 5000S of PTT (Thailand) was offered at VND 35,900/kg incl. VAT. This distributor's offers were reportedly about VND 1,000/kg below the general offer level from other suppliers.

In the north, the distributor for LSP offered HDPE blow of CP Chem (Singapore) origin at VND 35,500–36,100/kg incl. VAT depending on grade; one distributor offered HDPE Film FS7000 of STL (China) origin at VND 35,000/kg incl. VAT; HDPE Film 5604F of Formosa (Taiwan) at VND 34,000/kg incl. VAT at Hai Phong port; and HDPE pipe 23050 (PE100 group) of LyondellBasell (China) origin at VND 35,500/kg incl. VAT. Offer prices for HDPE yarn were also commonly ranging around VND 35,000–36,000/kg incl. VAT.

Another distributor offered HDPE blow 6200B of Iran origin at VND 36,500/kg incl. VAT. However, domestic buyers currently only accept HDPE blow at VND 32,000–33,000/kg incl. VAT, and higher offers have drawn little response from buyers.

For LLDPE, offers for LLDPE Film 7042/7042 from Chinese producers in the middle of this week saw some sellers raise prices sharply to VND 31,500–32,000/kg incl. VAT in the north for July–August delivery; prompt LLDPE Film 20211FE of Sibur was also offered at VND 31,500/kg incl. VAT; and LLDPE Film 218WJ of Sabic rose sharply to VND 32,500/kg incl. VAT in the north. Some sellers offered LLDPE Film of US and Canadian origin at VND 30,500/kg incl. VAT, but with distant delivery in September 2026. As of today, most sellers held prices flat.

Similarly, for LDPE, some traders offered LDPE Film of US origin at VND 38,000/kg incl. VAT. However, some sellers offered LDPE Radco and LDPE Film of Formosa (Taiwan) and Thai origin at just VND 36,000/kg incl. VAT. LDPE Film 260GG of Lotte Titan was offered again at VND 40,000/kg incl. VAT from midweek and is currently holding flat.

summary: US PVC to India 650-670 USD/ton CIF, lowest among all origins
SCG Thailand SP660/SG660 settled 720 USD/ton CIF (down 40)
Platts: China PVC +5 to 750; SE Asia +10 to 705 USD/ton CFR
paras: Global PVC supply is beginning to recover after the disruptions caused by the US–Iran conflict that began in March 2026, with more US-origin PVC now flowing to Asia.

US PVC offers to India this week stood at USD 650–670/ton CIF, currently the lowest among all international origins (Chinese-origin PVC was offered to India at USD 690–715/ton CIF; Thai and Indonesian PVC at USD 700–710/ton CIF; Korean PVC at USD 700–725/ton CIF; and Japanese PVC at USD 730/ton CIF).

In the Vietnamese market, today, 17 July, the distributor for SCG (Thailand) quoted firm selling prices for August 2026 PVC cargoes to domestic buyers. PVC grade SP660 was settled at USD 720/ton CIF and grade SG660 at USD 720/ton CIF, down USD 40/ton versus the previous firm selling price. This level is still seen as fairly high relative to buyers' expectations. Most customers were in no rush to commit, awaiting further August PVC offers from other major regional suppliers (expected in volume next week).

In domestic trading, PVC offers from distributors this week mostly held flat and have not yet “reversed” sharply higher like other resins. This is because domestic producer AGC Vina (ACVN) recently issued a separate quote to some large customers seeking early prices for August 2026 PVC cargoes, with PVC PM66R at just VND 18,800/kg excl. VAT (about VND 20,300/kg with 8% VAT included), down a sharp VND 1,500/kg versus the July cargo offer announced in mid-June.
On the commercial market, offer and transaction prices for PVC are commonly at VND 20,000–21,500/kg incl. VAT.
According to Platts assessments, PVC prices in China rose slightly by USD 5/ton this week to USD 750/ton CFR, while PVC prices in Southeast Asia rose USD 10/ton week-on-week to USD 705/ton CFR.

According to assessments from another international agency, PVC prices in Northeast Asia rose USD 10/ton week-on-week; PVC prices in Southeast Asia also rose USD 10/ton (to USD 700–740/ton CFR); and PVC prices in India and other South Asian countries ranged from flat to up USD 20/ton.

Asian PVC prices rose this week, supported by a sharp increase in feedstock costs, despite persistently weak demand across the region. Suppliers have been unable to raise PVC prices further to match the swing in feedstock costs, held back by subdued trading activity. Many coal-based PVC producers have cut operating rates.

Another notable development is that the Korea Trade Commission has launched an anti-dumping investigation into imports of suspension-grade polyvinyl chloride (S-PVC) from China into Korea. This will have a certain impact on Chinese PVC exports.
